title: Smoking Cessation and Health Promotion in COPD
subtitle: A 58-year-old with COPD and a 35 pack-year history is seen after a recent exacerbation and says he is ready to quit. The nurse uses the 5 A's, helps choose cessation medication, and updates prevention (lung screening, vaccines, pulmonary rehab).

patient.name: D. Hayes
patient.admitDate: Outpatient clinic. Follow-up after a recent COPD exacerbation (GOLD 3, 35 pack-years). States he is ready to quit smoking; here for cessation support and a health-promotion update.

labs.r: Set quit date after start; take with food.

labs.v: Avoid in seizure or eating disorder.

labs.r: Follow up within a week of quit date.

nursesNote: D. Hayes, 58, has COPD (GOLD 3) and a 35 pack-year history and is seen after a recent exacerbation, now stating he is ready to quit smoking. The nurse uses the 5 A's — asking about tobacco use, advising clearly to quit, assessing readiness (he is in the preparation stage), assisting with treatment, and arranging follow-up. For pharmacotherapy, varenicline is the most effective single agent (quit date set after starting, taken with food to limit nausea), combination NRT (a patch plus a short-acting form) is more effective than one alone, and bupropion is avoided in seizure or eating disorders. Behavioral counseling and a Quitline referral reinforce the plan, with follow-up within a week of the quit date. The visit also updates prevention: he qualifies for annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening (age 50-80, 20+ pack-years, currently smoking), receives pneumococcal and influenza vaccines with Tdap and shingles catch-up, is referred to pulmonary rehabilitation, and has cardiovascular risk addressed (blood pressure, lipids, statin if indicated).
providerOrders: Apply the 5 A's; advise quitting and assess readiness
Start cessation pharmacotherapy (varenicline or combination NRT); set a quit date
Provide behavioral counseling and a Quitline referral
Order annual low-dose CT screening; give COPD vaccines; refer to pulmonary rehab
Address cardiovascular risk; follow up within a week of the quit date

opts.text: Smoking is the leading preventable cause of death and worsens COPD
Quitting provides health benefits at any age
The 5 A's (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange) structure cessation counseling
Combining pharmacotherapy with behavioral counseling improves quit rates
A 58-year-old with a 35 pack-year history is too late to benefit from quitting
He qualifies for annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening
Varenicline is the most effective single cessation agent; set a quit date after starting
Bupropion is avoided in seizure disorders and eating disorders
Combination NRT (patch plus a short-acting form) is more effective than one alone
Adults with COPD should receive pneumococcal and influenza vaccines
NRT is more dangerous than continuing to smoke
Pulmonary rehabilitation improves quality of life and reduces hospitalizations
Ask about tobacco use and advise quitting clearly
Assess his readiness to quit
Assist: choose pharmacotherapy and set a quit date
Provide behavioral counseling and Quitline resources
Arrange follow-up within a week of the quit date
Offer varenicline or combination NRT with counseling
Order annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening
Give pneumococcal and influenza vaccines
Refer to pulmonary rehabilitation
Prescribe bupropion to a patient with a seizure disorder
Tell him it is too late to benefit from quitting
Recommend e-cigarettes as the preferred cessation method
Skip follow-up after setting the quit date

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

Start cessation pharmacotherapy and set a quit date
Provide behavioral counseling and a Quitline referral

Arrange follow-up within a week of the quit date

He is referred to pulmonary rehabilitation

Bupropion is prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder
He is told quitting now won't help
